analogy: Think of "Resources" through the chapter's own opening images: 3 Teacher’s Note The Indian Constitution has been regularly referred to in the previous two Social and Political Life textbooks. Unlike the previous two books, where little space was devoted to discussing the Constitution itself, this year the chapters in Unit 1 take the Constitution... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 1990 Constitution of Nepal 2015 Constitution of Nepal, GLOSSARY, Resources, Note The Indian Constitution. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Resources is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 3 Teacher’s Note The Indian Constitution has been regularly referred to in the previous two Social and Political Life textbooks.

The text explains that unlike the previous two books, where little space was devoted to discussing the Constitution itself, this year the chapters in Unit 1 take the Constitution as its main focus.

The text explains that chapter 1 begins with a discussion of the principles that underlie a liberal constitution.

The text explains that three short storyboards have been used to familiarise the student with the ideas that are being discussed.

The text explains that the storyboards use incidents located within a classroom-setting to illustrate three complex constitutive principles.

The text explains that the storyboards should be used to aid the student’s understanding of these constitutive principles.

The text explains that the discussion on the Indian Constitution is situated within a historical context.

The text explains that this has been done with the express intent that students become aware of the major influence that our anti-colonial struggle had on Indian democracy.

The text explains that in discussing the Constitution, we have had to use several new, and often difficult terms, to explain certain key features.

The text explains that while teaching these, please keep in mind that the student will continue to study these key features in greater depth in higher classes.

The text explains that therefore, the attempt here is to familiarise the student with a very basic understanding of the significance of these features within the working of democracy in India.

The text explains that the most prevalent definition of secularism is that it refers to the separation between Religion and the State.

The text explains that the chapter uses this definition as the foundation and then proceeds to elucidate two complicated ideas: the first points to why this separation is important and the second to what is particular to Indian secularism.

The text explains that there are two main reasons why the separation between Religion and State is important.

The text explains that the first is to prevent the domination of one religion over another, i.e. inter-religious domination.

The text explains that the second is to oppose the various types of domination that can happen within a religion, i.e. intra-religious domination.

The text explains that for example, the chapter discusses untouchability in Hindu religious practice which allowed ‘upper castes’ to dominate members of some ‘lower castes’.

The text explains that the second major conceptual idea that the chapter deals with is the unique nature of Indian secularism.

The text explains that the chapter explains this and refers to it as ‘principled distance’.

The text explains that this means that any interference in religion by the State has to be based on the ideals laid out in the Constitution.

The text explains that it is crucial that you understand these points clearly before teaching this chapter.

The text explains that it is very likely that students will come up with several suggestions for why the government should intervene or not intervene in religious affairs.

The text explains that the Indian Constitution and Secularism 2024-25

The text explains that social and Political Life 4 In this chapter, we are going to begin with football, a game many of you have probably heard of, or even played.

The text explains that like these games, a society also has constitutive rules that make it what it is and differentiate it from other kinds of societies.

The text explains that a written document in which we find such rules is called a Constitution.

The text explains that we have looked at the Indian Constitution in Classes VI and VII in our Social and Political Life textbooks.

The text explains that have you ever wondered why we need a Constitution or been curious about how the Constitution got written, or who wrote it?

The text explains that in this chapter, we will discuss both these issues and also look at the key features of the Indian Constitution.

The text explains that each of these features is crucial to the working of democracy in India and some of these will be the focus of different chapters in this book.

The text explains that 5 The Indian Constitution Today most countries in the world have a Constitution.

The text explains that while all democratic countries are likely to have a Constitution, it is not necessary that all countries that have a Constitution are democratic.

The text explains that or, put another way, a Constitution tells us what the fundamental nature of our society is.

The text explains that a Constitution helps serve as a set of rules and principles that all persons in a country can agree upon as the basis of the way in which they want the country to be governed.

The text explains that this includes not only the type of government but also an agreement on certain ideals that they all believe the country should uphold.

The text explains that between December 1946 and November 1949, the Constituent Assembly drafted a constitution for independent India.

The text explains that you will read more about the work of the Constituent Assembly later in the chapter.

The text explains that social and Political Life 6 Let us try and understand what we mean by this through two contrasting situations in the recent history of Nepal, a country that borders India on the north.

The text explains that the previous Constitution of Nepal, which had been adopted in 1990, reflected the fact that the final authority rested with the King.

The text explains that the people had to write a new Constitution to establish Nepal as a democracy.

The text explains that the reason that they did not want to continue with the previous Constitution is because it did not reflect the ideals of the country that they want Nepal to be, and that they have fought for.

The text explains that as in the game of football, in which a change in the constitutive rules will change the game altogether, Nepal, by moving from a monarchy to a democratic government, needs to change all its constitutive rules in order to usher in a new society.

The text explains that this is why, the people of Nepal adopted a new Constitution for the country in 2015.

The text explains that ideal: A goal or a principle in its most excellent or perfect form.

The text explains that indian national movement: The Indian national movement emerged in nineteenth- century India and saw thousands of men and women coming together to fight British rule.

The text explains that this culminated in India’s independence in 1947.

The text explains that you will learn about this in greater detail in your history textbook this year.

The text explains that polity: A society that has an organised political structure.

The text explains that sovereign: In the context of this chapter it refers to an independent people.

The text explains that human Trafficking: The practice of the illegal buying and selling of different commodities across national borders.

The text explains that in the context of Fundamental Rights discussed in this chapter, it refers to illegal trade in human beings, particularly women and children.

The text explains that tyranny: The cruel and unjust use of power or authority.

Source: NCERT Class 8 Resources and Development, Chapter 1: Resources

prelims_traps: Do not treat 1990 Constitution of Nepal 2015 Constitution of Nepal and GLOSSARY as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Resources; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

options: Social and Political Life 4 In this chapter, we are going to begin with football, a game many of you have probably heard of,...
As the name suggests, this is a game that involves the players’ feet.
According to the rules of football, if the ball touches the arm of any player (except the goalkeeper), then this is considered a foul.
So if players start holding the football in their hands and passing it around, then they are not playing football any more.

options: Similarly other games, such as hockey or cricket, also have rules according to which they are played.
Each of these rules helps define the game, and helps us distinguish one game from another.
As these are fundamental to the game, we can also call them the constitutive rules of the game.
Like these games, a society also has constitutive rules that make it what it is and differentiate it from other kinds of societies.
